name: Small Business Cybersecurity Assessment
description: A practical, plain-English cybersecurity assessment designed for small to medium-sized businesses (SMBs) without dedicated security teams. Covers the 10 most critical cybersecurity areas using accessible language. Based on CISA's Cybersecurity Performance Goals and NIST guidance for small businesses.

domains.name: 1. Account & Password Security
domains.description: Controls around how user accounts and passwords are managed across the organization.

controls.name: Strong password requirements enforced
controls.question: Does the organization require strong passwords for all accounts — at minimum 12 characters with complexity — and are these requirements technically enforced (not just a policy)?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Active Directory or Azure AD password policy screenshot, password manager deployment evidence, evidence of complex password requirements on all systems.
controls.why_it_matters: Weak passwords are cracked in seconds using modern tools. Strong passwords significantly increase the time and effort required to compromise an account.
common_weaknesses: Password policy exists but not enforced technically
Short passwords (< 8 characters) still allowed
Passwords never required to change when compromised
Same password used across multiple systems
controls.remediation_guidance: Set minimum password length to 14 characters. Enforce complexity via Group Policy or IdP. Deploy a corporate password manager (Bitwarden, 1Password, Keeper). Enable compromised password detection (Microsoft Entra ID Password Protection or HaveIBeenPwned integration).
controls.risk_impact: Password cracking, credential stuffing, unauthorized access.

scoring_guidance.3: Strong policy enforced with password manager
scoring_guidance.4: Strong policy, password manager, compromised credential monitoring

controls.name: Multi-factor authentication (MFA) for email and remote access
controls.question: Is MFA turned on and required for all email accounts and any remote access to company systems, and can it NOT be skipped?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace MFA enforcement evidence, VPN MFA configuration, remote desktop MFA evidence, list of any MFA exceptions.
controls.why_it_matters: MFA stops more than 99% of automated credential attacks. Email and remote access are the most common entry points for attackers.
common_weaknesses: MFA turned on but not required (users skip it)
Personal email accounts used for business without MFA
Remote desktop accessible without MFA
Only the owner/admin has MFA
controls.remediation_guidance: Enable and enforce MFA in Microsoft 365 (Conditional Access) or Google Workspace (2-Step Verification enforcement). Enable MFA for VPN and remote desktop. Do not allow exceptions without compensating controls.
controls.risk_impact: Account takeover, business email compromise (BEC), unauthorized remote access.

scoring_guidance.3: MFA required for all email and remote access
scoring_guidance.4: MFA enforced everywhere with no exceptions; phishing-resistant MFA for key accounts

controls.name: Former employee access revoked immediately
controls.question: When an employee leaves the organization, are all their accounts, email, and remote access disabled within the same business day?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Offboarding checklist, recent examples of offboarding (anonymized), HR-IT notification process documentation, account disable/delete audit log.
controls.why_it_matters: Former employees — especially those who left on bad terms — can cause significant harm using retained access. Immediate revocation is essential.
common_weaknesses: No formal offboarding IT checklist
Accounts disabled days or weeks after departure
Email forwarding left active after termination
Shared passwords not changed after personnel departure
controls.remediation_guidance: Create an offboarding checklist that IT signs off on same day as HR processes the departure. Use your IdP to disable all access from a single action. Forward email to manager for 30 days, then delete. Change shared passwords and service accounts the former employee had access to.
controls.risk_impact: Unauthorized access, data theft, sabotage by former employee.

scoring_guidance.4: Automated offboarding triggered by HR; access revoked instantly

domains.name: 2. Device Security
domains.description: Controls to protect company computers, laptops, phones, and other devices.

controls.name: Antivirus / endpoint protection on all devices
controls.question: Is up-to-date antivirus or endpoint protection software installed and active on all company computers, laptops, and servers?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Antivirus/EDR management console showing coverage and last update dates, policy requiring AV on all devices, mobile device management (MDM) evidence.
controls.why_it_matters: Antivirus and endpoint protection detect and block the majority of commodity malware before it can cause damage.
common_weaknesses: Some computers without AV
AV definitions outdated (> 24 hours)
AV disabled by users or excluded from scanning key areas
Personal devices used for work without AV
controls.remediation_guidance: Deploy Microsoft Defender for Endpoint (included in Microsoft 365 Business Premium) or a commercial EDR solution. Centrally manage and monitor. Set auto-update for definitions. Prevent users from disabling protection.
controls.risk_impact: Malware infection, ransomware, data exfiltration.

scoring_guidance.1: AV present but not managed centrally
scoring_guidance.2: AV on all devices, manually updated
scoring_guidance.4: EDR with behavioral detection, centrally managed, 100% coverage, alerts reviewed

controls.name: Automatic software updates enabled
controls.question: Are operating system and software updates set to install automatically (or on a defined schedule within 14 days of release for critical patches) on all company devices?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Windows Update or WSUS policy, Mac update management evidence, patch compliance report, mobile device update policy.
controls.why_it_matters: Attackers exploit known vulnerabilities, often within days of public disclosure. Automatic updates close these gaps quickly.
common_weaknesses: Users defer updates indefinitely
Updates only applied when IT remembers
Server patching neglected
Third-party software (Adobe, Chrome, Zoom) not included in patch management
controls.remediation_guidance: Enable automatic updates for Windows, macOS, and all major applications. Use Microsoft Intune, WSUS, or a patch management platform. Set critical patch deadline to 72 hours. Include third-party software.
controls.risk_impact: Exploitation of known vulnerabilities; ransomware via unpatched software.

scoring_guidance.4: Automated patch management with compliance reporting and SLAs for all software

controls.name: Hard drive encryption on laptops
controls.question: Are all company laptops encrypted so that if one is lost or stolen, the data on it cannot be read?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: BitLocker status report (Windows) or FileVault status (Mac), MDM encryption compliance report.
controls.why_it_matters: Laptops are stolen or lost regularly. Without encryption, the thief has full access to all files, emails, and saved passwords.
common_weaknesses: Encryption never enabled
Only some laptops encrypted
Encryption keys not backed up (risk of permanent data loss)
BYOD devices not required to encrypt
controls.remediation_guidance: Enable BitLocker on all Windows laptops and FileVault on all Macs. Back up recovery keys to Active Directory or Azure AD. Enforce via MDM (Intune, Jamf).
controls.risk_impact: Data breach from lost/stolen laptop; regulatory notification obligations; reputational damage.

scoring_guidance.3: All laptops encrypted with recovery keys backed up
scoring_guidance.4: All devices encrypted via MDM enforcement with compliance monitoring

domains.name: 3. Email Security
domains.description: Controls to protect the organization from email-based threats including phishing and business email compromise.

controls.name: Email security and anti-phishing controls
controls.question: Does the organization have email security controls beyond basic spam filtering, including anti-phishing, malicious link scanning, and attachment sandboxing?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Email security configuration (Microsoft Defender for Office 365, Google Workspace advanced protection, Proofpoint, Mimecast), anti-phishing policy settings, SPF/DKIM/DMARC DNS records.
controls.why_it_matters: Phishing is the #1 initial access vector for attacks. Advanced email security blocks sophisticated phishing emails that basic spam filters miss.
common_weaknesses: Basic spam filter only
No link scanning (users click malicious URLs)
Attachments not sandboxed
DMARC not configured (allows email spoofing)
controls.remediation_guidance: Enable Microsoft Defender for Office 365 Plan 1 or equivalent. Configure anti-phishing, safe links, and safe attachments policies. Set up SPF, DKIM, and DMARC for your domain. Consider a third-party email security gateway for additional protection.
controls.risk_impact: Phishing attacks succeed; ransomware delivered via email; business email compromise.

scoring_guidance.3: Full email security with sandboxing, DMARC enforced
scoring_guidance.4: Comprehensive email security with AI detection, user-reported phishing workflow, and regular simulations

controls.name: DMARC configured to prevent email spoofing
controls.question: Is DMARC configured for all company email domains with at minimum a 'quarantine' or 'reject' policy to prevent criminals from sending spoofed emails appearing to come from your domain?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: DMARC record lookup (mxtoolbox.com/dmarc), SPF record lookup, DKIM configuration evidence, DMARC reporting setup.
controls.why_it_matters: Without DMARC, criminals can send emails that appear to come from your domain, enabling supplier fraud, impersonation attacks, and BEC targeting your customers and partners.
common_weaknesses: No DMARC record
DMARC set to 'none' (monitoring only, does not block)
SPF record missing or incorrect
DKIM not configured
controls.remediation_guidance: Set up SPF, DKIM, and DMARC for all company domains. Start DMARC in monitoring mode (p=none) to identify legitimate email sources, then move to quarantine and finally reject. Use a DMARC management service if helpful.
controls.risk_impact: Brand impersonation, BEC targeting customers/partners, supplier fraud.

scoring_guidance.4: DMARC at reject with monitoring and reporting; all sending sources authorized

domains.name: 4. Data Backup & Recovery
domains.description: Controls to ensure business data can be recovered after a ransomware attack, hardware failure, or other disaster.

controls.name: Regular backups of all critical business data
controls.question: Are all critical business files, databases, and systems backed up automatically, and are backups stored in a separate location (not connected to the same network as production)?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Backup policy, backup schedule, backup tool/service used, most recent backup log showing success, evidence of offsite or cloud backup.
controls.why_it_matters: Backups are the only guaranteed way to recover from ransomware without paying. Backups connected to your network can also be encrypted by ransomware.
common_weaknesses: Backups are on the same network (ransomware can reach them)
Backups exist but restores never tested
Only file data backed up (not systems/configurations)
Cloud sync services (OneDrive, Google Drive) mistaken for backup
controls.remediation_guidance: Implement the 3-2-1 rule: 3 copies, 2 different media types, 1 offsite. Use an immutable cloud backup (Veeam, Azure Backup, Backblaze, Acronis). Ensure backups cannot be deleted by ransomware (immutability, separate credentials). Note: cloud sync is NOT a backup.
controls.risk_impact: Complete, unrecoverable data loss from ransomware; business closure; regulatory violations for data loss.

scoring_guidance.0: No backups or untested backups only
scoring_guidance.4: Immutable backups with automated restore testing and defined RTOs/RPOs

controls.name: Backup restores are regularly tested
controls.question: Has the organization actually tested restoring data from backups within the past 6 months to confirm that backups work and that data can be recovered in a reasonable timeframe?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Restore test records with dates and results, backup monitoring alerts, recovery time documentation.
controls.why_it_matters: Many organizations discover their backups are corrupted or cannot be restored only when they actually need them during a crisis.
common_weaknesses: Backups never tested
Only file-level restores tested (not full system)
Restore process too slow (RTO not met)
Backup integrity alerts not monitored
controls.remediation_guidance: Schedule quarterly restore tests. Test both file-level and full-system restores. Document how long restores take. Verify data integrity post-restore. Fix any issues found immediately.
controls.risk_impact: Discovery during a crisis that backups do not work; extended downtime; potential business failure.

scoring_guidance.4: Automated restore testing with alerts and documented RTOs verified

domains.name: 5. Network & Wi-Fi Security
domains.description: Controls to protect the organization's network from unauthorized access and threats.

controls.name: Firewall protecting internet connection
controls.question: Is a properly configured firewall in place between the internet and company systems, with unnecessary inbound ports closed and outbound traffic filtered?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Firewall make/model, last configuration review date, inbound access rules, evidence of unused port closure, firmware version.
controls.why_it_matters: A properly configured firewall is the first line of defense against internet-based attacks. Without one, company systems may be directly accessible from the internet.
common_weaknesses: Consumer-grade router used as business firewall
Default firewall configuration never hardened
Unnecessary inbound ports open (RDP, SMB)
Firewall firmware never updated
controls.remediation_guidance: Deploy a business-grade firewall (Fortinet, Cisco Meraki, SonicWall, Palo Alto). Adopt a deny-by-default policy for inbound traffic. Close unnecessary ports especially RDP (3389), SMB (445), and Telnet (23). Keep firmware current.
controls.risk_impact: Direct exposure of systems to internet attacks; exploitation of open ports for ransomware delivery.

scoring_guidance.4: Next-gen firewall with IPS, URL filtering, and active management

controls.name: Separate guest Wi-Fi network
controls.question: Is the company's Wi-Fi network password-protected with WPA2 or WPA3, and is there a separate guest network that cannot access internal company systems?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Wi-Fi configuration screenshots, SSID list, VLAN configuration, guest network isolation evidence.
controls.why_it_matters: Guest or personal devices on the same Wi-Fi as company systems can be used to attack company resources and can observe company network traffic.
common_weaknesses: Guest and company devices on same Wi-Fi network
WEP or WPA encryption (outdated/breakable)
Default or weak Wi-Fi password
Wi-Fi password shared with everyone including visitors
controls.remediation_guidance: Create separate SSIDs for company and guest use. Ensure guest network cannot reach internal systems (network isolation). Use WPA2/WPA3 with a strong password. Change company Wi-Fi password when employees leave.
controls.risk_impact: Unauthorized network access; attacks on company systems from guest devices; eavesdropping on company traffic.

scoring_guidance.3: Isolated guest network, WPA3, strong password management
scoring_guidance.4: Network segmentation with WPA3-Enterprise and certificate-based authentication

domains.name: 6. Employee Security Awareness
domains.description: Controls to ensure employees can recognize and respond appropriately to cybersecurity threats.

controls.name: Annual cybersecurity training for all employees
controls.question: Do all employees complete cybersecurity awareness training at least once per year, covering phishing, social engineering, password safety, and what to do if they suspect a breach?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Training platform enrollment and completion records, training curriculum, date of most recent training cycle, completion rate.
controls.why_it_matters: Employees who cannot recognize a phishing email are the most commonly exploited entry point. Training is proven to reduce click rates significantly.
common_weaknesses: Training only at hiring, never repeated
Generic training employees click through without reading
No training verification (quiz, test)
Completion rates below 80%
controls.remediation_guidance: Implement an online security awareness training platform (KnowBe4, Proofpoint Security Awareness, Microsoft Viva Learning, or free SANS courses). Require annual completion. Add a quiz with minimum passing score. Track completion.
controls.risk_impact: Phishing and social engineering attacks succeed at high rates; negligent data handling; ransomware via email.

scoring_guidance.4: Continuous training, role-specific modules, phishing simulations with metrics

controls.name: Phishing simulation and reporting culture
controls.question: Does the organization run simulated phishing tests to measure and improve employee ability to recognize phishing emails, and is there an easy way for employees to report suspicious emails?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Phishing simulation platform evidence, simulation results over time (click rate trend), report phishing button or email address, incident log showing reports acted on.
controls.why_it_matters: Real-world measurement through simulations identifies which employees need more training before they fall for a real attack. A reporting culture enables faster response when real phishing occurs.
common_weaknesses: No phishing simulations
Simulations performed but no remedial training for clickers
No way for employees to report suspicious emails
Employees fear punishment for clicking (kills reporting culture)
controls.remediation_guidance: Implement quarterly phishing simulations. Auto-enroll clickers in targeted training (not punishment). Add a 'Report Phishing' button in email (Microsoft Report Message add-in or equivalent). Acknowledge and thank employees who report real phishing.
controls.risk_impact: High phishing susceptibility; real attacks succeed; delayed detection when employees do not report suspicious activity.

scoring_guidance.3: Quarterly simulations with remedial training and formal reporting button
scoring_guidance.4: Continuous simulation program with metrics, trend tracking, and a mature reporting culture

domains.name: 7. Vendor & Third-Party Security
domains.description: Controls to manage the cybersecurity risk posed by vendors, suppliers, and third-party service providers who have access to your systems or data.

controls.name: Vendor access review and management
controls.question: Does the organization maintain an inventory of third-party vendors with access to company systems or data, and are vendor access rights reviewed regularly and revoked when the relationship ends?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Vendor inventory list, vendor access agreements or contracts with security requirements, evidence of vendor access reviews, termination/offboarding process for vendors.
controls.why_it_matters: Third-party vendors are one of the most common paths into organizations (supply chain attacks). Many organizations have more vendor accounts than they realize.
common_weaknesses: No vendor inventory
Vendor accounts never reviewed or removed
Vendors have broad access not limited to what they need
No contracts requiring vendors to notify of breaches
controls.remediation_guidance: Inventory all vendors with system/data access. Require minimum necessary access (least privilege). Review vendor access quarterly. Immediately remove access when a vendor relationship ends. Require vendors to notify you of security incidents affecting your data.
controls.risk_impact: Vendor account compromise leading to your data breach; supply chain attacks; unauthorized access through vendor credentials.

scoring_guidance.3: Inventory with access reviews and contracts with security requirements
scoring_guidance.4: Formal vendor risk program with assessments, contracts, and monitored access

domains.name: 8. Incident Response
domains.description: The organization's ability to respond to and recover from cybersecurity incidents.

controls.name: Basic incident response plan
controls.question: Does the organization have a written plan describing what to do if a cyberattack, data breach, or ransomware attack occurs — including who to call first and what actions to take immediately?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Incident response plan document, emergency contact list (IT, legal, insurer), any evidence of plan review or test.
controls.why_it_matters: Organizations without a plan make costly, avoidable mistakes during incidents: paying ransoms unnecessarily, destroying evidence, missing notification deadlines, or taking actions that worsen the situation.
common_weaknesses: No written plan
Plan exists but contact list is outdated
No cyber insurance or insurer contact not known
Data breach notification requirements not understood
controls.remediation_guidance: Write a simple one-page incident response plan. Include: who to call (IT contact, CEO, legal counsel, cyber insurer), immediate steps to take (disconnect infected devices, preserve evidence), and notification requirements (state breach laws, HIPAA if applicable). Review and test annually.
controls.risk_impact: Disorganized response; increased damage and downtime; missed legal notification deadlines; regulatory penalties.

scoring_guidance.3: Written plan with contacts, reviewed annually
scoring_guidance.4: Written and tested plan with tabletop exercise, insurer notified, legal counsel identified

controls.name: Cyber insurance coverage
controls.question: Does the organization maintain cyber liability insurance to cover costs associated with data breaches, ransomware, business interruption, and legal notification obligations?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Cyber insurance policy, coverage amounts (first-party and third-party), incident response retainer coverage, evidence that policy requirements are met.
controls.why_it_matters: Cyber incidents can cost hundreds of thousands of dollars in recovery, legal fees, and notification costs. Cyber insurance provides financial protection and often includes IR support.
common_weaknesses: No cyber insurance
General business insurance assumed to cover cyber (it usually does not)
Cyber insurance obtained but policy requirements (MFA, backups) not met — may void coverage
Coverage limits too low for the organization's risk
controls.remediation_guidance: Obtain standalone cyber liability insurance. Review coverage for ransomware, business interruption, and third-party liability. Ensure you understand and meet all policy requirements (MFA, backup requirements). Work with a broker specializing in cyber.
controls.risk_impact: Catastrophic uninsured financial loss from cyber incident.

scoring_guidance.3: Adequate coverage with IR retainer, requirements understood and met
scoring_guidance.4: Comprehensive coverage reviewed annually; policy requirements verified and compliant

domains.name: 9. Physical Security
domains.description: Controls to protect physical access to systems, devices, and sensitive information.

controls.name: Physical access controls to servers and networking equipment
controls.question: Is physical access to servers, network equipment, and other critical infrastructure limited to authorized personnel only, and is physical access logged or monitored?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Server room access log or keycard records, lock/badge system evidence for server room, visitor log, evidence of locked equipment cabinets.
controls.why_it_matters: Physical access to servers and network equipment can bypass all logical security controls. An attacker with physical access can extract data, install keyloggers, or compromise systems directly.
common_weaknesses: Server room unlocked or accessible to non-IT staff
No log of who has accessed server room
Network switches accessible in open areas (closets, conference rooms)
Visitors allowed in server room unescorted
controls.remediation_guidance: Secure servers in a locked room or cabinet with keycard or combination lock access. Log physical access. Mount network equipment in locked cabinets. Require IT escort for any visitor near critical infrastructure.
controls.risk_impact: Physical data theft, hardware tampering, keylogger installation, bypass of network security controls.

scoring_guidance.2: Locked room with limited key access
scoring_guidance.4: Badge access, camera, access log reviewed, visitor escort policy

controls.name: Clean desk and screen lock policies
controls.question: Do employees lock their screens when leaving their desks, and is sensitive information secured rather than left visible on desks or screens?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Clean desk policy, screen lock policy, screen lock timeout configuration (GPO or MDM), evidence of policy communication.
controls.why_it_matters: Sensitive information visible on screens or desks can be seen by visitors, other employees, or captured in photos. Unlocked screens allow unauthorized access if someone walks up to an unattended computer.
common_weaknesses: Screens left unlocked and unattended for hours
Sensitive documents left on desks overnight
No screen lock timeout configured
Whiteboard containing sensitive information visible to visitors
controls.remediation_guidance: Configure automatic screen lock after 5–10 minutes via Group Policy or MDM. Train employees on Win+L (Windows) or Control+Command+Q (Mac) to instantly lock. Implement a clean desk policy requiring sensitive documents secured at end of day.
controls.risk_impact: Unauthorized access to open systems; sensitive data visible to unauthorized individuals.

scoring_guidance.3: Auto-lock configured (≤ 5 min) with clean desk policy
scoring_guidance.4: Auto-lock + clean desk + tailored physical access + policy verified

domains.name: 10. Cloud & SaaS Security
domains.description: Controls to secure cloud services and software-as-a-service applications used by the organization.

controls.name: Cloud service inventory and configuration review
controls.question: Does the organization maintain an inventory of all cloud services (Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, AWS, Salesforce, etc.) in use, and are security settings reviewed to ensure they are configured securely?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Cloud service inventory, Microsoft Secure Score or equivalent, cloud configuration review evidence, admin account list for each cloud service.
controls.why_it_matters: Shadow cloud services (unknown to IT) and misconfigured cloud settings are among the most common causes of data breaches. Default cloud settings are often insecure.
common_weaknesses: Cloud services deployed by employees without IT knowledge (shadow IT)
Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace on default settings
External sharing enabled broadly for cloud storage
No visibility into SaaS apps connected to corporate accounts
controls.remediation_guidance: Use Microsoft Defender for Cloud Apps or similar to discover shadow IT. Review Microsoft Secure Score and improve configurations. Restrict external sharing to approved partners. Require IT approval for new SaaS deployments.
controls.risk_impact: Data exposed via misconfigured cloud storage; shadow IT as unknown breach vector; unauthorized data sharing.

scoring_guidance.4: Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM) with automated compliance checking

controls.name: Secure Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace configuration
controls.question: Is the organization's primary cloud productivity suite (Microsoft 365 or Google Workspace) configured with recommended security settings including MFA, anti-phishing, conditional access, and data loss prevention?
controls.evidence_guidance: Request: Microsoft Secure Score or Google Workspace security health checkup, Conditional Access or BeyondCorp policy configuration, anti-phishing policy, DLP policy evidence, admin account audit.
controls.why_it_matters: Microsoft 365 and Google Workspace are the most targeted platforms for attackers. Default settings leave many dangerous configurations enabled.
common_weaknesses: Microsoft 365 on default settings (Security Score < 50%)
Global Admin accounts used for daily tasks
External email auto-forwarding allowed
No audit logging enabled or reviewed
Excessive OAuth app permissions granted
controls.remediation_guidance: Work through Microsoft Secure Score recommendations. Enable: MFA for all users, Conditional Access policies, Safe Links and Safe Attachments, external forwarding block, unified audit logging, and admin MFA. Review and restrict OAuth application permissions.
controls.risk_impact: Email account takeover, data exfiltration via auto-forwarding rules, Business Email Compromise.

scoring_guidance.4: Secure Score > 85%, all CIS benchmark recommendations implemented, actively monitored
